name: Register-Based vs Stack-Based Bytecode
summary: The two dominant bytecode designs trade code size against dispatch count. Stack-based (JVM, CPython, Wasm) keeps instructions tiny but multiplies dispatch; register-based (Lua 5, Dalvik, V8 Ignition) widens instructions but cuts ops by ~47% (Shi VEE 2008). Modern dynamic-language VMs favor register; Wasm and managed runtimes keep stack for verification compactness.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
The two dominant bytecode designs make different trade-offs between code size and dispatch count.

**Stack-based** (JVM, CPython, WebAssembly, CLR): operations pop operands from an implicit stack and push results. Instructions are small (often 1 byte opcode + minimal operands) because operand locations are implicit. The downside: each sub-computation requires separate push/pop instructions, inflating instruction count.

**Register-based** (Lua 5, Dalvik, V8 Ignition, LuaJIT): operations reference named virtual registers. Instructions are wider (typically 3–4 bytes with opcode + operand fields), but the same computation uses far fewer instructions.

sections.title: Empirical trade-offs
sections.content:
Ierusalimschy's "The Implementation of Lua 5.0" (2005) reported a 50% reduction in executed instructions when Lua switched from stack to register bytecode, yielding significant interpreter speedups despite larger bytecode.

Shi et al. (VEE 2008, "Virtual Machine Showdown: Stack Versus Registers") measured the trade-off precisely: register-based VMs execute ~47% fewer VM instructions but their code is ~25% larger. On modern CPUs, dispatch cost often dominates, so many dynamic-language VMs have favored register bytecode since Lua 5 and Dalvik.

WebAssembly and several managed runtimes remain important counterexamples: stack discipline can enable compact encoding, single-pass validation, and structured control flow that are worth more than dispatch reduction in their target environments.

sources.label: Virtual Machine Showdown (Shi et al., VEE 2008)

sections.title: Design implications
sections.content:
For a dynamic-language VM optimising for interpreter throughput, register bytecode is the modern default. The wider instructions cost cache space but win in dispatch count, and modern branch predictors handle register-bytecode dispatch well.

For a verifiable bytecode (Wasm, JVM under the original verifier), stack discipline simplifies the validator: stack-effect tracking is a small state machine over each instruction, while register tracking requires more elaborate liveness reasoning. Wasm's structured control flow plus stack discipline allows single-pass validation that Wasm's design point requires.

For a JIT-only language (where bytecode is just a hand-off between frontend and JIT), the choice matters less because the bytecode is barely interpreted. JIT compile time and IR translation cost dominate.
